articles.title: heatwave red alert remains in chhatarpur with temperatures above 41°C - The News Mill
articles.search_term: heatwave ("Agar Malwa" OR Alirajpur OR Anuppur OR Ashoknagar OR Balaghat OR Barwani OR Betul OR Bhind OR Bhopal OR Burhanpur OR Chhatarpur OR Chhindwara OR Damoh OR Datia OR Dewas OR Dhar OR Dindori OR Guna OR Gwalior OR Harda OR Hoshangabad OR Indore OR Jabalpur OR Jhabua OR Katni OR Khandwa OR Khargone OR Mandla OR Mandsaur OR Morena OR Narsinghpur OR Neemuch OR Panna OR Raisen OR Rajgarh OR Ratlam OR Rewa OR Sagar OR Satna OR Sehore OR Seoni OR Shahdol OR Shajapur OR Sheopur OR Shivpuri OR Sidhi OR Singrauli OR Tikamgarh OR Ujjain OR Umaria OR Vidisha) Madhya Pradesh
articles.full_text:
A red alert for heatwaves continues in Chhatarpur district, Madhya Pradesh, as temperatures have surpassed 41°C since early on Sunday, May 24. Nearby areas such as Khajuraho and Nowgong have experienced temperatures as high as 47.4°C in recent days, with severe heatwave conditions persisting.
The extreme heat has led to deserted local markets as residents remain indoors, stepping outside only for essential activities. Many are attempting to mitigate the effects of the intense heat by drinking water and sugarcane juice.
Dilip Kshatriya, a local resident, said, “There is a continuous red alert for a heatwave in the district. If we talk about the temperatures, it has crossed 41°C since early morning, making it extremely difficult for people to step out of their houses. People are only leaving their homes for highly essential or urgent work.”
He also stated, “The severity of the heatwave is such that a complete silence has taken over the local weekly markets (haat-bazaars). To protect themselves from this scorching weather, people are continuously drinking water and sugarcane juice to quench their thirst. The severe impact of the weather can be felt everywhere.”
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast that temperatures in the national capital will reach 44°C today. The IMD advises the public to avoid prolonged exposure to direct sunlight, stay hydrated, and limit outdoor activities during peak afternoon hours.
Recommendations include consuming seasonal fruits such as watermelon, cucumber, and cantaloupe, as well as leafy vegetables. The IMD suggests drinking shikanji and lassi instead of caffeine, alcohol, and sugary beverages to help maintain lower body temperatures.
Many residents have adopted protective measures, with people in urban areas commonly using umbrellas, wide-brimmed hats, and fabric scarves to shield themselves from direct solar radiation while outdoors.
